title: A Guide to Onboarding a New Sales Rep

interlinking_suggestions.title: Thailand's Long-Term Resident (LTR) Visa for Professionals
How to Hire Your First Salesperson
How to Create a Sales Playbook for Your SaaS Team

author.name: Chloé Dubois
author.bio: Chloé is a communications expert who coaches freelancers on the art of client management. She writes about negotiation, project management, and building long-term, high-value client relationships.

key_takeaways: To avoid creating a taxable presence abroad (Permanent Establishment risk), legally restrict your international sales contractor's authority to soliciting orders, ensuring all contracts are signed by your home entity.

Prevent severe tax and legal penalties by correctly classifying international hires, understanding that authorities prioritize the 'Right to Control' you exert over the relationship, not the title in the contract.
Protect your company's cash flow by designing commission structures that pay out only after customer payment is received, not merely when a deal is signed.
Systematize your 'founder magic' into a scalable asset by creating a 'Minimum Viable Playbook' that documents your ideal customer, core messaging, and battle-tested objection handlers.

Implement a structured 30-60-90 day plan that moves a new hire from knowledge certification in month one to guided execution in month two and finally to autonomous, metric-driven ownership in month three.
summary: Hiring your first salesperson without a proper foundation creates significant legal and financial risks that can cripple your company. To succeed, founders must first de-risk the hire with compliant contracts and cash-flow-protective commissions, then systematize their sales knowledge into a repeatable playbook. This framework, combined with a structured 30-60-90 day plan, empowers your new hire to become an autonomous asset, transforming the sales function into a scalable engine for growth.
ai_overview_answer: To build a scalable sales engine with a first hire, founders should follow a three-phase process focused on de-risking, systematizing, and empowering. Before the hire's first day, it is critical to mitigate legal and financial liabilities by correctly classifying the hire as a contractor or employee and neutralizing Permanent Establishment (PE) tax risk with a carefully drafted contract. This foundational contract must limit the hire's authority to bind the company and should be paired with a commission structure that pays only after cash is received. Next, systematize your sales process by creating a "Minimum Viable Playbook" containing your ideal customer profile, value proposition, and objection handling. Record your own sales calls to provide a library of proven examples for training. Finally, implement a structured 30-60-90 day plan to transition the hire from knowledge certification to guided execution, and ultimately to full ownership of their pipeline. This framework ensures the new hire is empowered to operate the sales system independently, transforming them into a predictable driver of revenue.
meta_title: Onboarding a New Sales Rep: Build a Scalable Sales Engine
meta_description: Don't just train your first hire. Our guide to onboarding a new sales rep shows founders how to de-risk, systematize, and build a true sales engine. Get the playbook.

itemListElement.name: A Guide to Onboarding a New Sales Rep

@graph.name: How to Onboard a New Sales Rep
@graph.headline: A Guide to Onboarding a New Sales Rep
@graph.description: Hiring your first salesperson without a proper foundation creates significant legal and financial risks that can cripple your company. To succeed, founders must first de-risk the hire with compliant contracts and cash-flow-protective commissions, then systematize their sales knowledge into a repeatable playbook. This framework, combined with a structured 30-60-90 day plan, empowers your new hire to become an autonomous asset, transforming the sales function into a scalable engine for growth.

step.name: De-Risk the Foundation Before Day One
step.text: Before you draft a single line of training material, you must build a legal and financial bulwark around your business. Getting this wrong creates liabilities that can destroy your company before your new hire closes their first deal.

itemListElement.text: Master the Contractor vs. Employee Distinction: The first critical step, especially when hiring internationally, is correctly classifying the relationship. Misclassifying an employee as an independent contractor is a business-ending mistake that can trigger massive back taxes, penalties, and liability for benefits.

Neutralize Permanent Establishment (PE) Risk: When you hire a sales contractor in another country, you can inadvertently create a Permanent Establishment (PE). This is a tax concept where your contractor's activities create a taxable presence for your business in their country, making you liable for corporate taxes there.

Craft a Bulletproof International Contractor Agreement: Your contract is your primary shield against both misclassification and PE risk. A generic template is dangerously insufficient. Your agreement must be a precise legal instrument.

Design a Commission Structure That Protects Cash Flow: How you structure compensation is not just about motivation; it's about survival. For a founder-led business, cash flow is king. The superior, cash-flow-protective approach is to pay commissions after the client has paid their invoice.

step.name: Systematize Your 'Founder Magic' into a Scalable Playbook
step.text: With your legal and financial risks ring-fenced, you can now translate the sales success that lives inside your head into a system someone else can execute. The challenge is to download your tacit knowledge—your process, your vision, your 'founder magic'—into a repeatable system. This is how you begin to clone yourself.

itemListElement.text: Build the 'Minimum Viable Playbook' First: Resist the urge to write a 100-page sales bible. Your goal is speed and utility. Create a Minimum Viable Playbook (MVP) that gives your new hire the core information they need to be effective from week one.

Become the Subject of Your Own Training Manual: You are the primary source material. To build an authentic training program, you must systematically record and analyze your own successful actions. Use a tool like Loom or Zoom to record yourself conducting a full sales cycle.
Create a Centralized 'Single Source of Truth': To prevent every question from coming to you, create a 'Single Source of Truth' (SSoT)—one centralized, organized location for all sales-related assets.

Define the Non-Negotiables of Your Brand: Your personal ethos is the company culture. How you sell is as important as what you sell. Document the operational rules of engagement that protect your brand.

step.name: The 30-60-90 Day Plan for Empowering Autonomy
step.text: A playbook without a timeline is just a wish. You must translate those assets into a structured, metrics-driven framework for progressively delegating ownership. The goal is not to manage a person, but to empower them to operate your sales system independently, freeing you to focus on strategy.

itemListElement.text: Days 1-30: Controlled Immersion & Knowledge Certification: The priority for the first 30 days is learning. The new rep’s only job is to become an expert on your company, product, customer, and process. They should not carry a revenue target.
Days 31-60: Guided Execution & Process Adherence: With a certified foundation of knowledge, the rep moves from theory to practice with significant oversight. Your role is to be the guide rails.

Days 61-90: Delegated Ownership & Performance Accountability: This is where true autonomy begins. Having demonstrated knowledge and process adherence, the rep earns ownership of a territory, vertical, or lead source.

Establish a Feedback Cadence That Builds Confidence: A common mistake is to blend coaching with performance management. Separate them to reduce anxiety and increase effectiveness with weekly tactical check-ins and monthly strategic meetings.

mainEntity.name: What are the biggest legal risks when hiring a foreign sales contractor?

acceptedAnswer.text: The two primary dangers are employee misclassification and creating Permanent Establishment (PE) risk. Misclassification can expose you to massive liabilities for back taxes and benefits. PE risk is when your contractor's activities (like closing deals) create a taxable presence for your company in their country. A meticulously drafted contract is your most important defense against both.

mainEntity.name: As a solo founder, what is the high-level process for onboarding my first sales rep?

acceptedAnswer.text: It's a three-stage process: 1) De-Risk: Solidify the legal and financial groundwork with a compliant contract and a cash-flow-positive commission plan before they start. 2) Systematize: Translate your 'founder magic' into a tangible Minimum Viable Playbook. 3) Empower: Use a structured 30-60-90 day plan to transition them from learning to full, metrics-driven ownership.

mainEntity.name: How do I create a sales playbook from scratch when I'm the only salesperson?

acceptedAnswer.text: Start with a 'Minimum Viable Playbook.' Focus only on the essentials: your Ideal Customer Profile (ICP), value proposition, messaging for top pain points, and answers to common objections. Then, record yourself conducting calls and demos using a tool like Loom. These real-world videos are the fastest way to build an authentic and actionable training library.

mainEntity.name: What's the best way to train a new sales rep without a sales team?

acceptedAnswer.text: Use a four-step progression: 1) Playbook Immersion: They must master the playbook first. 2) Passive Shadowing: They listen in on your live calls. 3) Role-Playing: You play the prospect in mock calls to build their confidence in a safe environment. 4) Reverse Shadowing: You shadow their first live calls silently, providing immediate feedback afterward.

mainEntity.name: How can I prevent my international contractor from creating Permanent Establishment (PE) risk?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most critical step is to structure their authority. Your contractor must not have the power to 'conclude contracts' on behalf of your company. Their role should be explicitly defined as soliciting orders, which are then formally accepted and signed by you in your home country. This separation of duties must be enshrined in their contract.

mainEntity.name: What key clauses must be in an international sales contractor agreement?

acceptedAnswer.text: While you must consult with a legal professional, ensure your agreement contains: a precise Statement of Work, a clear Independent Contractor Status clause, an IP ownership clause, a robust Confidentiality (NDA) section, detailed Payment & Commission Terms, and a critical Limitation of Authority clause stating they cannot legally bind your company.

body.content: Hiring your first salesperson is a monumental act of trust. You’re handing someone the keys to your company's growth engine. But most founders, driven by an urgent need for revenue, fixate on sales training and ramp time. They skip the one phase that can shield their business from catastrophic failure: de-risking the foundation.

This is not another guide on sales onboarding. This is a three-phase playbook for transforming your first hire from a potential liability into your company’s most valuable asset. We will move from building a legal and financial fortress to systematizing your "founder magic," and finally, to empowering true autonomy. This is how you stop being the business and start building one.
Phase 1: De-Risk the Foundation Before Day One

Before you draft a single line of training material, you must build a legal and financial bulwark around your business. Getting this wrong creates liabilities that can destroy your company before your new hire closes their first deal.
Master the Contractor vs. Employee Distinction

The first critical step, especially when hiring internationally, is correctly classifying the relationship. Misclassifying an employee as an independent contractor is a business-ending mistake that can trigger massive back taxes, penalties, and liability for benefits.
Globally, tax authorities use “Right to Control” tests to determine the true nature of the relationship. The core difference lies in the level of control you exert. Ask yourself:

items: **Behavioral Control:** Do you dictate *how, when, and where* the work is done? If you set work hours, require specific processes, or provide mandatory training on task completion, they look like an employee.

**Financial Control:** Do you reimburse business expenses or provide essential equipment like a laptop? An independent contractor invests in their own tools and has the opportunity to realize a profit or loss.

**Relationship Type:** Is the relationship intended to be permanent? Is the salesperson performing a function that is core to your business operations? A revenue-generating role is often seen as integral, increasing the risk of an employee determination.

body.content: A mistake here isn't a minor issue; it's a foundational threat.
Neutralize Permanent Establishment (PE) Risk

When you hire a sales contractor in another country, you can inadvertently create a **Permanent Establishment (PE)**. This is a tax concept where your contractor's activities create a taxable presence for your business in their country, making you liable for corporate taxes there. This risk is most acute when a sales representative has the authority to regularly negotiate and conclude contracts on your company's behalf.
As international tax lawyer David Crewe warns, this is a frequent and costly oversight:

The single biggest mistake a founder can make is assuming that because their company is young, or because they don't have a physical office in a country, they are exempt from its tax laws. This oversight can lead to significant retroactive tax liabilities and penalties that can cripple a growing business.
body.author: David Crewe, international tax lawyer

body.content: To neutralize this threat, the contractor's authority must be limited to soliciting orders. All contracts must be formally accepted and signed by you or your entity in your home country.
Craft a Bulletproof International Contractor Agreement

Your contract is your primary shield against both misclassification and PE risk. A generic template is dangerously insufficient. Your agreement must be a precise legal instrument that contains these critical clauses:

items: **Statement of Independent Contractor Status:** Explicitly state that the hire is an independent contractor responsible for their own taxes and insurance, and is not entitled to employee benefits.

**Limitation of Authority:** Clearly define that the contractor has no authority to legally bind your company, sign contracts, or represent themselves as an employee. This is your key defense against PE risk.
**Intellectual Property (IP) Ownership:** Specify that all work product, customer lists, and proprietary information developed during the engagement belong exclusively to your company.
**Confidentiality (NDA):** Protect your sensitive business information with a strong non-disclosure clause.
**Clear Termination Conditions:** Outline the specific terms under which either party can end the relationship, providing a clean exit strategy.

body.content: Design a Commission Structure That Protects Cash Flow

How you structure compensation is not just about motivation; it's about survival. For a founder-led business, cash flow is king. The most common mistake is paying commissions when a deal is signed. The superior, cash-flow-protective approach is to pay commissions *after* the client has paid their invoice.

rows: **High.** You pay out cash you haven't received. If the client pays late or never, you've lost money.
Motivates reps to close deals quickly.
**Low.** You only pay commissions from revenue you actually have. Protects from bad debt and clawbacks.
Aligns the sales rep's success directly with the company's financial health.

body.content: This structure ensures you are only paying for collected revenue, transforming the sales function from a potential liability into a self-funding growth engine.
Phase 2: Systematize Your "Founder Magic" into a Scalable Playbook

With your legal and financial risks ring-fenced, you can now translate the sales success that lives inside your head into a system someone else can execute. The common advice to "shadow a senior rep" is useless when *you are* the senior rep. The challenge is to download your tacit knowledge—your process, your vision, your "founder magic"—into a repeatable system. This is how you begin to clone yourself.
Build the "Minimum Viable Playbook" First

Resist the urge to write a 100-page sales bible. Your goal is speed and utility. Create a **Minimum Viable Playbook (MVP)** that gives your new hire the core information they need to be effective from week one. Focus only on the highest-leverage components:

items: **A Crystal-Clear Ideal Customer Profile (ICP):** Who do you sell to and, just as importantly, who do you *not* sell to? Define the titles, industries, company sizes, and trigger events that signal a perfect fit.
**Your Core Value Proposition:** In one or two sentences, what is the primary outcome you deliver for your ICP?
**Key Messaging for the Top 3 Pain Points:** Document the exact language you use to discuss the three most significant problems your product solves.
**Answers to the 5 Most Common Objections:** You know the pushback you get on every call. Write down the questions and your proven, battle-tested responses.

body.content: Become the Subject of Your Own Training Manual
You are the primary source material. To build an authentic training program, you must systematically record and analyze your own successful actions.

Use a tool like Loom or Zoom to **record yourself** conducting a full sales cycle. Capture a discovery call, a product demo, and a pricing negotiation. These recordings are gold because they demonstrate tonality, pacing, and how you navigate difficult questions in real-time. Transcribe your best calls to analyze word choice. Instead of just telling your new hire what to do, you're giving them a library of proven success to model.
Create a Centralized "Single Source of Truth"

As a founder, you are the ultimate bottleneck. To prevent every question from coming to you, create a **"Single Source of Truth" (SSoT)**—one centralized, organized location for all sales-related assets.

This doesn't need to be complex. A well-structured Google Drive folder or a dedicated Notion workspace is perfect. The key is discipline. Every asset a sales rep might need must live here: the playbook, call recordings, email templates, case studies, product documentation, and pricing sheets. An SSoT empowers your new hire to find their own answers, fostering the independence and problem-solving skills they need to succeed.
Define the Non-Negotiables of Your Brand

Your personal ethos *is* the company culture. How you sell is as important as what you sell. A new sales rep is an ambassador for your brand, and their interactions must align with the reputation you've built. Document the operational rules of engagement that protect your brand:

items: **Tone & Voice:** Is your brand's communication style formal and expert-driven, or casual and relational? Provide examples.
**Discounting Philosophy:** When, if ever, is it acceptable to offer a discount? What is the approval process? Define the guardrails to protect your margins and perceived value.
**Customer Interaction Principles:** What is your core philosophy for handling tough customer conversations or feature requests you can't meet?

body.content: Defining these principles ensures that as the sales function scales, your brand's integrity and customer experience scale with it.
Phase 3: The 30-60-90 Day Plan for Empowering Autonomy

A playbook without a timeline is just a wish. You must translate those assets into a structured, metrics-driven framework for progressively delegating ownership. The goal is not to manage a person, but to empower them to operate your sales system independently, freeing you to focus on strategy.
Days 1-30: Controlled Immersion & Knowledge Certification
The priority for the first 30 days is learning. The new rep’s only job is to become an expert on your company, product, customer, and process. They should not carry a revenue target.

items: **Key Activities:** Study the playbook, listen to your recorded calls, shadow your live calls, and practice using the CRM with dummy accounts.

**The "Certification" Test:** At the end of 30 days, the rep must "certify" by successfully pitching you. Can they articulate the value proposition? Can they handle your toughest objections? This proves knowledge retention.
**Primary Metric:** **Knowledge Retention.** Success is measured by their ability to pass the certification test.

body.content: Days 31-60: Guided Execution & Process Adherence
With a certified foundation of knowledge, the rep moves from theory to practice with significant oversight. Your role is to be the guide rails.

items: **Key Activities:** Begin prospecting a controlled set of leads. Draft all prospect communications for your review before sending. Conduct their first live calls with you shadowing them silently.
**Primary Metric:** **Strict Adherence to the Sales Process.** Are they following the playbook? Are they using the CRM correctly? You are measuring the *how*, not just the outcome.

body.content: Days 61-90: Delegated Ownership & Performance Accountability

This is where true autonomy begins. Having demonstrated knowledge and process adherence, the rep earns ownership of a territory, vertical, or lead source. Your role evolves from micro-manager to strategic coach.

items: **Key Activities:** Independently manage their assigned pipeline from lead to close. Take responsibility for their own forecasting and reporting.
**Primary Metrics:** **Lead-to-Close Conversion Rate & Initial Revenue Targets.** Performance is now tied to tangible business outcomes.

body.content: Establish a Feedback Cadence That Builds Confidence
A common mistake is to blend coaching with performance management. Separate them to reduce anxiety and increase effectiveness.

items: **Weekly Tactical Check-in (30 min):** Focus purely on the numbers. Review the dashboard, discuss the pipeline, and solve immediate roadblocks. This is about performance.
**Monthly Strategic Meeting (60 min):** Dedicate this time to coaching and development. Review call recordings together and ask how you can provide better support. This is about growth.

body.content: This dual-cadence approach ensures performance is managed consistently while creating dedicated space for the coaching that fosters long-term success.
Frequently Asked Questions
What are the biggest legal risks when hiring a foreign sales contractor?

The two primary dangers are **employee misclassification** and creating **Permanent Establishment (PE) risk**. Misclassification can expose you to massive liabilities for back taxes and benefits. PE risk is when your contractor's activities (like closing deals) create a taxable presence for your company in their country. A meticulously drafted contract is your most important defense against both.
As a solo founder, what is the high-level process for onboarding my first sales rep?

It's a three-stage process: 1) **De-Risk:** Solidify the legal and financial groundwork with a compliant contract and a cash-flow-positive commission plan before they start. 2) **Systematize:** Translate your "founder magic" into a tangible Minimum Viable Playbook. 3) **Empower:** Use a structured 30-60-90 day plan to transition them from learning to full, metrics-driven ownership.
How do I create a sales playbook from scratch when I'm the only salesperson?

Start with a **"Minimum Viable Playbook."** Focus only on the essentials: your Ideal Customer Profile (ICP), value proposition, messaging for top pain points, and answers to common objections. Then, record yourself conducting calls and demos using a tool like Loom. These real-world videos are the fastest way to build an authentic and actionable training library.
What's the best way to train a new sales rep without a sales team?

Use a four-step progression: 1) **Playbook Immersion:** They must master the playbook first. 2) **Passive Shadowing:** They listen in on your live calls. 3) **Role-Playing:** You play the prospect in mock calls to build their confidence in a safe environment. 4) **Reverse Shadowing:** You shadow *their* first live calls silently, providing immediate feedback afterward.
How can I prevent my international contractor from creating Permanent Establishment (PE) risk?

The most critical step is to structure their authority. Your contractor must **not** have the power to "conclude contracts" on behalf of your company. Their role should be explicitly defined as soliciting orders, which are then formally accepted and signed by you in your home country. This separation of duties must be enshrined in their contract.
What key clauses must be in an international sales contractor agreement?

While you must consult with a legal professional, ensure your agreement contains: a precise Statement of Work, a clear Independent Contractor Status clause, an IP ownership clause, a robust Confidentiality (NDA) section, detailed Payment & Commission Terms, and a critical **Limitation of Authority** clause stating they cannot legally bind your company.
Conclusion: Your First Hire Is Not an Employee—It's Your First System

The process of onboarding your first salesperson is the single most important act of systemization you will undertake as a founder. This isn't about adding headcount or delegating tasks. It's a fundamental shift from being the business's engine to becoming its architect.

Many founders view their first hire as just a pair of hands, keeping themselves as the permanent bottleneck. The approach detailed here is designed to prevent that fate. It forces you to treat your first hire as the first component of a much larger machine: your scalable revenue system.
Think of the phases as the deliberate construction of that machine:

items: **Phase 1 (De-Risking)** was about building the chassis. The meticulous focus on compliance, contracts, and cash-flow-positive commissions creates a stable foundation that can withstand the pressures of growth.

**Phase 2 (Systematizing)** was about engineering the engine. By creating a sales playbook, you translated your intuitive "founder magic" into a documented, transferable process—the operating manual for the machine.

**Phase 3 (Empowering)** was about handing over the keys. The 30-60-90 day plan is a structured ignition sequence, designed to transfer ownership safely and empower your new rep to operate the system with confidence.

body.content: By embracing this mindset, you accomplish something far more profound than just hiring a salesperson. You build an asset. You create a repeatable, predictable engine for generating revenue that adds tangible value to your company—value that exists separately from your own personal effort. This is how you transform your Business-of-One into a business that can truly scale.
